topic: The inside of my wedding band is engraved with a date six years before I met my wife and the jeweler has a record of the original engraving in my own name
title: My Wedding Ring Is Engraved With a Date Six Years Before I Met My Wife. The Jeweler Has the Original Order. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I had my wedding band resized last week after it slipped off in the laundry. The jeweler buffed the inside of the band before resizing it and an older engraving emerged that I had never seen before. The engraving reads June fourteen, two thousand ten, with my initials and the initials M.R. I did not meet my wife until two thousand sixteen. The jeweler pulled the original order and the band was bought under my own name eight years before I ever set foot in that store.
description:
I had my wedding band resized last week after it slipped off in the laundry. The jeweler buffed the inside of the band before resizing it and an older engraving emerged that I had never seen before. The engraving reads June fourteen, two thousand ten, with my initials and the initials M.R. I did not meet my wife until two thousand sixteen. The jeweler pulled the original order and the band was bought under my own name eight years before I ever set foot in that store.

I ordered the band through a Tiffany flagship in two thousand eighteen and had it engraved with my wedding date March nine, two thousand nineteen. It has lived in my dresser drawer for the eight years since. When I called Tiffany customer service to flag the manufacturing error, the representative pulled the original order under my membership number. The first order on file was placed June twelve, two thousand ten. It was billed to my own American Express, a card I did not open until two thousand twelve. The shipping address was an apartment in Madison, Wisconsin. I have never lived in Madison.

My mother remembers the apartment. She remembers coming with me to pick out the ring. She remembers the girl I bought it for. She texted me a photograph that night. There is a woman standing beside me in the picture, holding my left hand. The face is my wife's face. The photograph was taken six years before I met her. Watch to the end. The engraving has been changing.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I had my wedding band resized last week. I had lost a little weight over the winter and the band had slipped off my finger in the laundry. I drove the ring to a small jewelry shop two blocks from my office on a Tuesday afternoon. The jeweler buffed the inside of the band on a felt wheel to clean the surface before resizing. He stopped the wheel and looked at me strangely. He angled a desk lamp over the bench and asked me to come around to his side of the counter. There was an engraving on the inside of the band that I had never seen before. The engraving read June fourteen, two thousand ten. My initials. The initials M.R. I did not meet my wife until two thousand sixteen.

sections.narration: The shop is called Hartwell Jewelers. It sits in a row of brick storefronts on a side street I pass every morning on my way to work. The owner is an older man named Daniel who has run the bench for thirty one years. The front of the shop is a single long display case with two velvet pads and a small chair for customers. The back of the shop is the workbench where Daniel does the resizing and the soldering. I had come in on a Tuesday afternoon because the shop is quietest then. My ring had slipped off into the washing machine on Sunday and my wife had caught it before the cycle finished. I had driven home with the band in my coat pocket and decided that morning to have it resized properly.

sections.narration: I had bought the band in two thousand eighteen. I had ordered it through the Tiffany flagship downtown six months before our wedding. The salesperson had walked me through three weights of plain gold band. I had picked the middle weight. I had paid for the custom engraving on the inside of the band with the date of our wedding. March nine, two thousand nineteen. My initials. My wife's initials. I had picked the ring up two weeks before the wedding. The box was robin's egg blue with a white ribbon. I had stored the box on the top shelf of my dresser. After the wedding I had moved the box to the bottom drawer. The band itself had lived on my left hand for eight straight years. I had never once taken it off to look inside.

sections.narration: Daniel took a macro photograph of the inside of the band with a small camera mounted above the bench. He pulled the image up on a tablet and zoomed in slowly. The image was sharp enough to count the tooling marks on the gold. There were two engravings on the inside of the band. The deeper one was the older. The deeper one read June fourteen, two thousand ten. My initials. The initials M.R. The lighter one was layered over the deeper one in shallower strokes. The lighter one was my actual wedding date. March nine, two thousand nineteen. My initials. My wife's initials. Daniel told me the deeper engraving was the original. He told me the shallower engraving had been added later, on top of the first, by a different hand and a different tool.

sections.narration: I drove home with the ring in a small paper envelope in my coat pocket. I told myself three things. One. Tiffany must have used a display band for the custom engraving and reused an older sample that had once been engraved for a different customer. Two. The original engraving had been buffed down at the time of the resale and missed a layer that only deep buffing would reveal eight years later. Three. The matching initials were a coincidence. Tens of thousands of men in my country share my exact first and last initials. The band weight and gold purity were standard. The simplest explanation was a quiet manufacturing reuse. I would call Tiffany customer service in the morning. I would ask them to pull the original order under my account. I would have an explanation by lunchtime.

sections.narration: I called Tiffany customer service the next morning. I gave the representative my membership number. She pulled the order history. She told me the most recent order on the account was the band I had bought in two thousand eighteen. She told me there was an older order on the same membership number. The first order on file under my name was dated June twelve, two thousand ten. One engraved gold band. Ready for pickup June fourteen, two thousand ten. The engraving on file read my initials and the initials M.R. The card used to pay was an American Express in my name ending in the same four digits as the card in my wallet. I told her that was not possible. I did not open that American Express until two thousand twelve. The card number had been mine for fourteen years.

sections.narration: I opened my Gmail archive and searched for the word Tiffany. The results loaded slowly. The most recent results were the order confirmations from two thousand eighteen. Beneath those was a confirmation email dated June twelve, two thousand ten. The subject line read your custom order is ready for pickup. The body of the email named one engraved gold band. The engraving listed my initials and the initials M.R. The shipping address on the order was an apartment on East Gorham Street in Madison, Wisconsin. I have never lived in Madison. I have never been to Wisconsin. I opened my sent folder and searched the same address. There was a thread from two thousand ten between me and the Tiffany account manager. The replies were from my exact account. The writing voice in the replies was mine.

sections.narration: I called my mother that night. I asked her if she remembered me living in Madison. She said yes. She said I had moved there for graduate school in the fall of two thousand nine. She said she had visited the apartment on East Gorham Street twice. She said the second visit was in June of two thousand ten. She said she had come to help me pick out a ring. I asked her what ring. She said the ring I had bought for the girl I was going to propose to that summer. I asked her the girl's name. She said the name without hesitating. She said the name was Marlowe. She told me I had picked up the band at the Tiffany flagship downtown on a Monday afternoon and she had been standing beside me when I did.

sections.narration: My mother texted me a photograph an hour after the call. I opened it on the couch. The image was scanned from an old print. The colors were warm and slightly faded. I was standing in the photograph at twenty one years old. I was wearing a coat I still owned. My hair was longer. The gold band was on my left ring finger. There was a young woman standing beside me. She was holding my left hand in both of hers. Her face was exactly the same as my wife's face. The same eyes. The same line of the jaw. The same small scar above the left eyebrow. She looked older in the photograph than my wife had been when I met her in two thousand sixteen. Behind us was an apartment window. The window framed a snowfield I had never seen in person.

sections.narration: I took the ring off that night. I put it inside a small metal tin and carried the tin down to my basement workshop. I left the tin on the top shelf above the bench. My wife asked me at breakfast why I had stopped wearing the band. I told her it needed resizing again. She watched me over her coffee for a long second before nodding. I went down to the basement after she left for work. I opened the tin under the bench lamp and lifted the band out with a pair of soft tweezers. The engraving on the inside had been updated. It now read June fourteen, two thousand ten. Three sets of initials. The first pair was mine and Marlowe's. The third pair was S.M. I do not know anyone with the initials S.M.
